mindMap.mermaidCode:
mindmap
  root((CE 323: Mass-Storage Systems - Chapter 11))
    Introduction
        Easiest chapter, 2-hour lecture
        Exam distribution in 3rd hour
        Objectives
            Describe physical structure of secondary storage devices
            Explain performance characteristics of mass-storage devices
            Evaluate I/O scheduling algorithms
            Discuss operating-system services (including RAID)
    HDD Fundamentals and Performance
        Overview of Mass Storage Structure
            Bulk of secondary storage
            Importance going down for general use
            Gaining importance for data centers, especially with AI growth
            Authors considered discarding topic due to "out of date" perception
            Still used in laptops and high-capacity storage
        Physical Structure of Hard Disk
            Spinal Platters
                Magnetically-coated material
                Rotate an angular motion
                Quite fast: 60 to 250 times per second
            Arm Assembly
                Monolithic structure
                Magnetic Heads (read-write heads)
                Heads not in physical contact with platter
                Disk crash if contact (damage from dropping laptop)
            Cylinder Concept
                Equidistant / Equicentric tracks
                Across multiple platters at the same radius
                Purpose: Minimize arm movement frequency
                Mechanism: Read/write as much data as possible per movement
                Storage sequence: Fill one cylinder, then change to another
        Performance Characteristics
            Transfer Rate
                Rate at which data flows between drive and computer
                Important: Must match speed of reading bits by head
                Otherwise, accumulation of buffered data leads to delays
                Theoretical: ~6 Gb/sec
                Effective/Real: ~1 Gb/sec
            Access Time Components (Delay Constraints)
                Positional Delay (Seek Time)
                    Time to move disk arm to desired cylinder
                    Dominating factor in I/O time
                    Many heads move all together
                    Range: 3ms - 12ms (9ms common for desktop)
                Rotational Delay (Rotational Latency)
                    Time for desired sector to rotate under disk head
                    Depends on spindle speed (RPM)
                    Average latency = ½ * (60 / RPM)
                Transfer Time
                    Time to read data and transfer to RAM
                Controller Overhead
            Disk Blocks / Sectors
                Smallest addressable unit for transfer
                Block size is equal to sector size
            Average I/O Time Calculation
                Average access time = average seek time + average latency
                Average I/O time = average access time + (amount to transfer / transfer rate) + controller overhead
    Solid State Drives and Memory Management
        Non-Volatile Memory (NVM) as a whole
            Solid State Disks (SSDs) are among them
            Also includes USB drives, DRAM disk replacements
        Solid State Disks (SSDs)
            Differences from HDDs
                IO nature drastically changes
                No moving parts (no seek time or rotational latency)
                Much faster
                Can be more reliable
                More expensive per MB
                Shorter lifespan (need careful management)
                    Limited erase cycles (~100,000)
                    Life span measured in Drive Writes Per Day (DWPD)
                Less capacity (typically)
            Challenges
                Read/written in "page" increments
                Erased in larger "block" increments
                Cannot overwrite in place (must erase first)
            Connectivity
                Buses can be too slow -> connect directly to PCI for example
    Disk Scheduling and Storage Management
        HDD Scheduling Algorithms
            Determine "whose I/O request goes next"
            Optimize disk access
            Minimize seek time, maximize bandwidth
        NVM Scheduling
        Error Detection and Correction
            Essential topic
        Storage Device Management
        Swap-Space Management
            Swap space is part of this discussion
        Storage Attachment
            Interface to the machine
        RAID Structure
